entry.title: _index
entry.content:
Public Housing in Western Australia

Articles in this topic cover the history, legislation, and practice of public housing in Western Australia — from the Workers' Homes Board through to contemporary social housing policy and Aboriginal housing access.

Articles

Article Description
Public Housing in Western Australia Conceptual overview: WA public housing from 1912 to the present
Workers Homes Act 1911 Analysis of the founding legislation establishing the Workers' Homes Board
Sharp 1993 - A History of Public Housing in Western Australia Sharp's thesis examining the Workers' Homes Board and State Housing Commission
Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010 The EOC's six-year monitoring report on discriminatory practices in public housing provision to Aboriginal people; substantially expanded 2026-05-08

Related Topics

  • Homelessness in Western Australia
  • Housing Economics

entry.title: Workers Homes Act 1911
entry.content:
Workers Homes Act 1911

The Workers' Homes Act 1911 (No. 8 of 1912) established the Workers' Homes Board in Western Australia. Despite its 1911 title, the Act was gazetted in 1912 — a minor piece of timing that Sharp (1993) notes is a minor source of confusion in the historical record.

The Act was not a social housing programme in any modern sense. It was a homeownership assistance scheme, targeted at working men with modest incomes. The state's interest was in settling a mobile, resource-dependent workforce.

Who it covered

Eligibility was income-tested. Workers earning up to £300 per year could apply. This excluded the genuinely poor — people below consistent wage employment — and also the more prosperous. It was designed specifically for the respectable working class: regularly employed, financially manageable, family-oriented.

What it offered

The Act provided two mechanisms. Workers could lease land from the Crown on a perpetual lease at 3% per annum. They could also take out a state-backed advance for a dwelling, up to £550, repayable over 30 years at 5% per annum. If repayments were made punctually, the interest rate dropped to 5.5% — an early version of discount-for-compliance logic.

Advances were secured by mortgage at 6% nominal (5.5% with the rebate), which the Board held over the property. Workers built or bought their homes; the state held security and received repayments. The transaction was closer to a building society than a housing charity.

The Workers' Homes Board

The Board administered the scheme. Sharp (1993) describes it as operating under the overlapping pressures of workforce housing demand from industry, housing demand from low-income earners, and the state's broader interest in population growth and economic development. The Board was not insulated from these pressures — it responded to them, adjusting loan terms and eligibility as conditions changed.

The physical size and climatic variation of WA also shaped the Board's operations. Housing in the north required different materials and construction approaches than in Perth. Decentralised administration was a practical necessity, not an ideological choice.

What it wasn't

The Act didn't offer public rental housing. It didn't serve Aboriginal Western Australians. It didn't cover agricultural workers or domestic workers. The workers it assisted were already in a position to sustain regular loan repayments — the most marginalised were excluded from the outset.

Sharp (1993) uses this to anchor a larger argument about housing as social control: the scheme rewarded respectable workforce participation and homeownership as a form of social settlement. Access to it was a privilege extended to productive citizens, not a right granted to residents.

Legacy

The Workers' Homes Board operated from 1912 until it was replaced by the State Housing Commission after World War II. The shift reflected changed conditions — postwar housing shortage, mass migration, and a political environment in which public rental housing had become viable at scale. But the underlying logic the Board had established — that housing access should be earned, managed, and conditional — persisted in how the Commission operated.

See Public Housing in Western Australia for the longer arc, and Sharp 1993 - A History of Public Housing in Western Australia for Sharp's full analysis.

Key Takeaways

  • The Workers' Homes Act 1911 (WA) created a homeownership loan scheme, not a social housing programme.
  • Eligibility was restricted to workers earning ≤ £300/year — it excluded both the very poor and the prosperous.
  • Land was available on perpetual lease at 3% p.a.; dwelling advances up to £550 at 5% over 30 years.
  • The scheme was designed for workforce settlement, not welfare.
  • Sharp (1993) reads it as an early instance of housing-as-social-control: conditional access tied to respectability and productive employment.

Related

  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Sharp 1993 - A History of Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Housing Economics

Sources

  • Workers' Homes Act 1911 (No. 8 of 1912) (WA).
  • Sharp, M. (1993). A History of Public Housing in Western Australia: The Workers' Homes Board and State Housing Commission. Unpublished thesis.

entry.title: Sharp 1993 - A History of Public Housing in Western Australia
entry.content:
Sharp 1993 — A History of Public Housing in Western Australia

Sharp's 1993 thesis examines public housing in WA through two organisations: the Workers' Homes Board (established 1912) and the State Housing Commission (post-WWII, trading as Homeswest from 1985). It is primarily a history of what shaped these organisations rather than a standard institutional account — Sharp is more interested in the forces acting on the system than in the bureaucratic detail.

Central argument

The thesis makes a specific claim worth holding onto: public housing in WA functioned as an instrument of social control. Access was structured as a reward — for workforce participation, for respectable household behaviour, for financial reliability — rather than as a right conferred by need or residence. Sharp doesn't frame this as a conspiracy. It was the operating logic of the system, embedded in eligibility criteria, allocation decisions, and tenancy management.

That said, Sharp doesn't reduce the history to top-down control. There is also evidence throughout the thesis that tenant agency and community values shaped what the organisations actually did. Clients pushed back. Communities organised. Policy responded, sometimes. The relationship between the state and its housing clients was not simply one of control and compliance.

Three driving pressures

Sharp identifies three overlapping factors that shaped how both organisations operated:

Industry demand for workforce housing. WA's economy was built on resource extraction and agriculture, both of which required mobile, temporary, or regional labour. Housing was a practical necessity for attracting and retaining workers. The state provided it because industry needed it.

Demand from low-income earners. There was persistent unmet demand for affordable housing from people who couldn't access the private market. Both organisations responded to this, though housing for low-income earners was always secondary to the workforce and economic development priorities.

The state's interest in population and economic growth. WA was chronically underpopulated relative to its land area and resource potential. Public housing was part of how the state attracted and settled migrants, particularly in the postwar period when migration programmes brought large numbers of Europeans to Perth and regional centres.

Material factors

Sharp also gives weight to things that don't fit neatly into a political economy argument. The physical size of WA, its climatic variation, and the material constraints of the postwar period all shaped what was built, where, and for whom. Decentralisation wasn't just a policy choice — it was a necessity in a state where major regional centres are several hundred kilometres from Perth.

Conclusion

Sharp's overall assessment of the state's role is positive, cautiously. Public housing in WA contributed to economic growth, provided infrastructure for industry, housed postwar migrants, and gave affordable accommodation to low-income families. It did real things. But it also controlled, excluded, and rationed — and the thesis is honest about that.

The tension Sharp identifies between housing as social control and housing as social provision runs through the entire history. It doesn't resolve.

Key Takeaways

  • Sharp frames WA public housing as shaped by workforce demand, low-income need, and population/economic growth — not primarily by welfare intent.
  • The central thesis is that housing access functioned as social control: a conditional reward rather than a right.
  • Tenant agency and community values also shaped the system — it wasn't purely top-down.
  • Sharp's assessment of the state's role is net positive, while acknowledging the exclusions.
  • The Workers' Homes Board (1912) and State Housing Commission (post-WWII) are treated as a single continuous story.

Related

  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Workers Homes Act 1911
  • Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010

Sources

  • Sharp, M. (1993). A History of Public Housing in Western Australia: The Workers' Homes Board and State Housing Commission. Unpublished thesis.

entry.title: Public Housing in Western Australia
entry.content:
Public Housing in Western Australia

Public housing in Western Australia has a long history, stretching from a modest worker loan scheme established in 1912 through to the large-scale social housing system that exists today. It has never been a simple story of the state providing a welfare safety net. The system was shaped by competing pressures — economic development, population growth, workforce housing demand, and later the specific and often discriminatory treatment of Aboriginal people.

The Workers' Homes Board (1912–1947)

The Workers Homes Act 1911 established the Workers' Homes Board in 1912. It was not, in any meaningful sense, a welfare programme. Workers earning up to £300 a year could apply for state-backed loans to build or buy a home — land on perpetual lease at 3% per annum, dwelling repayments at 5% over 30 years, with advances capped at £550. The state was in the business of helping working men into homeownership, not providing tenure.

Sharp (1993) frames the Board as driven by three overlapping demands: industry's need for workforce housing, low-income earners' need for shelter, and the state's interest in population and economic growth. Housing was a tool, and access to it reflected that. The Board's clients had some influence over how it operated — Sharp finds evidence that community values and tenant agency shaped policies — but the underlying logic was housing as a reward for productive workers, not as a right.

The State Housing Commission (1947–1985) and Homeswest (1985–)

After World War II the Workers' Homes Board was replaced by the State Housing Commission, which operated in a context of acute housing shortage and mass migration. The Commission built at scale, attracted migrants with housing promises, decentralised Perth-based bureaucracy, and provided rental housing to low-income households. Sharp's assessment is that the state's role was, on balance, positive — it genuinely housed people who couldn't otherwise afford accommodation.

In 1985 the Commission rebranded as Homeswest. The institutional name changed but the fundamental tensions did not: demand consistently outstripped supply, eligibility criteria remained a rationing mechanism, and access was never straightforward for the state's most marginalised residents.

Aboriginal Access and Discrimination

The most documented failure of the WA public housing system is its treatment of Aboriginal people. In 2004 the Equal Opportunity Commission conducted a formal inquiry — the first of its kind in Australia — into discriminatory practices in the provision of public housing and related services to Aboriginal people in WA. The resulting report, Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010, found systemic problems across staffing, communication, policy design, tenancy management, and the appeals process.

The follow-up implementation and monitoring committee, reporting in 2010, tracked what the Department of Housing had and hadn't changed. Progress was uneven. Employment of Aboriginal staff increased but remained low at senior levels. Interpreter services improved in some regions. Eviction rates for Aboriginal tenants remained disproportionate in several regions well into 2010.

The EOC inquiry didn't find individual bad actors so much as a system that hadn't been designed with Aboriginal people in mind, and hadn't been reformed enough to compensate.

Housing as Social Control

Sharp's (1993) central argument is worth sitting with: throughout the history of both the Workers' Homes Board and the State Housing Commission, housing functioned as social control. Allocation was a reward, not a right. Tenants and applicants who didn't conform to expected norms — in behaviour, in household structure, in appearance of financial responsibility — were disadvantaged or excluded.

That logic didn't disappear when the Commission became Homeswest. The EOC inquiry thirty years later found it still operating in the ways the Department managed debt, evictions, and appeals.

Key Takeaways

  • Public housing in WA began as a worker homeownership scheme in 1912, not a welfare programme.
  • The Workers' Homes Board prioritised workforce housing and population growth over housing as a social right.
  • The State Housing Commission (post-WWII) and its successor Homeswest expanded the system significantly but retained rationing logic.
  • The EOC inquiry (2004, final report 2010) found systematic discrimination against Aboriginal people across the full range of public housing services.
  • Sharp's (1993) thesis — housing as social control rather than right — remains a useful frame for understanding who gets housed and who doesn't across the full period.

Related

  • Workers Homes Act 1911
  • Sharp 1993 - A History of Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010
  • Homelessness in Western Australia
  • Housing Economics

Sources

  • Sharp, M. (1993). A History of Public Housing in Western Australia: The Workers' Homes Board and State Housing Commission. Unpublished thesis.
  • Workers' Homes Act 1911 (No. 8 of 1912) (WA).
  • Equal Opportunity Commission. (2010). Finding a Place: Final Report of the Implementation and Monitoring Committee. Perth: EOC.

entry.title: Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010
entry.content:
Finding a Place — EOC Report 2010

Finding a Place is the final report of the Implementation and Monitoring Committee for the Section 80 Inquiry into discriminatory practices in the provision of public housing to Aboriginal people in Western Australia. The EOC released the original inquiry in December 2004, after Acting Commissioner Moira Rayner initiated a formal investigation into the Department of Housing and Works following a wave of discrimination complaints from Aboriginal tenants and applicants between 1998 and 2002. The 2010 document covers six years of implementation tracking — whether the Department actually did what the EOC recommended.

The short version: some things changed. The conditions that produced unequal outcomes mostly did not.

Background

The 2004 inquiry found indirect race discrimination — conditions and requirements that were harder for Aboriginal people to comply with than non-Aboriginal tenants, not through stated intent but through effect. Overcrowding from extended family obligations, limited familiarity with bureaucratic processes, and lower English literacy rates meant that policies designed to be universal were not, in practice, equally accessible.

165 recommendations came out of the 2004 inquiry across five areas: staffing, communication, policy and practices, maintenance and tenancy management, and appeals. The Implementation and Monitoring Committee — government agencies, community organisations, individual Aboriginal community members — met every two months from 2005 to October 2010 to track progress.

Reform Area 1 — Staffing

The Accommodation Manager role was the main structural change. At the time of the inquiry, one position carried both tenancy enforcement and technical maintenance, creating a relationship with tenants built mostly around conflict. The Department split it into two: Housing Services Officers (HSOs) for tenancy support, Property Services Officers (PSOs) for maintenance. HSOs ended up with an average of 350 tenancies each — private sector property managers typically handle 150–200.

Aboriginal staffing in mid-2010: 7.35% of the Department's workforce, against 21.74% of tenants. Staff were concentrated at lower levels — 11.24% at Level 2 (Customer Service Officers) — and nearly absent from management grades. Cultural awareness training was introduced at induction with a four-year refresh cycle. The Committee considered four years too long, the training insufficiently embedded in practice, and the evaluation mechanisms inadequate. These concerns produced Recommendations 4 and 5 in the 2010 report.

The derogatory internal term "Fund 6" used to refer to Aboriginal housing was abolished.

Reform Area 2 — Communication

Standard letters, policy fact sheets, and the Departmental website were rewritten in plain English. A sub-committee of the Monitoring Committee reviewed the core letter suite — property offers, rent arrears notices, breach letters, eviction notices. Colour coding was introduced so tenants could gauge the seriousness of correspondence without reading every word.

Interpreter services were a consistent failure. The correlation between regions with high Aboriginal tenancy and regions where an Aboriginal language is the primary home language is direct: in the Kimberley, 70.84% of Department tenants are Aboriginal, and a substantial proportion don't speak English as a first language. The EOC released a formal report on the need for an Indigenous Interpreter Service in July 2010. During the same period, the Pilbara interpreter service closed.

The Department introduced a centralised 1300 number for maintenance requests. In regional and remote areas this produced two problems: many Aboriginal tenants found ringing an anonymous 1300 number culturally alien compared to face-to-face contact, and for tenants using mobile phones rather than fixed lines, the call was not free. The report speculates — reasonably — that these barriers meant maintenance requests weren't being lodged, and that tenant liability was accruing as a result.

Regional consultation expanded during the period: 176 meetings or open forums at regional/district level between 2008 and 2010, attended by 863 Aboriginal tenants.

Reform Area 3 — Policy and practices

Several concrete policy changes were made over the six years.

Identification requirements were broadened. Letters from community Elders became an accepted form of identification, acknowledging that many Aboriginal people don't hold drivers' licences or passports.

Old debts: statute-barred debts older than six years, where the Department had not previously sought recovery, were quashed. A debt discount scheme was introduced and publicised to current and prospective tenants.

Annual surveys: the old practice of removing applicants from the waiting list for failing to respond was replaced with a three-contact requirement before any removal. Regional advocates still reported in 2010 that some offices weren't following the new process.

Private rental: the requirement for Aboriginal applicants to prove attempts at the private rental market before being considered for priority housing was abolished. A 2009 EOC inquiry into private rental had confirmed that race discrimination was occurring; requiring Aboriginal people to demonstrate repeated rejection before accessing public housing was both ineffective and humiliating.

Secondary homelessness was a significant unresolved issue. The Rental Policy Manual still distinguished between primary homelessness and secondary homelessness (staying with family or friends), with the latter requiring proof that the arrangement couldn't continue before priority assistance would be granted. The report describes the structural problem plainly: Aboriginal families take in relatives as a cultural obligation. This produces overcrowding in existing tenancies, which in turn produces tenant liability charges, anti-social behaviour complaints from neighbours, and increased maintenance demands. The 2010 report recommended removing the primary/secondary distinction from policy altogether.

The priority housing waiting list grew significantly during the period. In 2004, around 700 people were on the priority list, with a wait of four to six months. By 2010, there were over 3,000 applicants, with average wait times exceeding two years. Around 40% of priority allocations between 2005 and 2008 went to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander clients. By December 2010, 80% of all Department allocations were coming from the priority list rather than the wait-turn list.

Reform Area 4 — Maintenance and tenancy management

A maintenance review completed in 2009 formalised response timeframes: emergency within 3 hours, priority within 48 hours, routine within 14 days. Completed work on jobs over $400 required PSO sign-off. A centralised phone registration system was introduced.

Evictions remained disproportionate. Bailiff evictions by year, against Aboriginal tenants' 21.74% share of the tenant population:

Year ATSI share of bailiff evictions
2006 35%
2007 49%
2008 47%
2009 49%
2010 (to June) 63%

The primary driver of terminations was rental arrears. An AHURI study (Beer, 2006) found that only 7% of evictions resulted from bailiff or police action — most tenants left after receiving a termination notice or in anticipation of one. In the first half of 2010, 1,518 termination notices were issued across the state. The Caretaker database couldn't break these down by reason; the figures had to be calculated manually.

Anti-social behaviour procedures were revised: the definition was narrowed to exclude trivial complaints (children running across lawns, noise a private landlord wouldn't normally act on). Tenants were entitled to be informed of allegations and given the opportunity to respond. The Disruptive Behaviour Management Strategy, announced by the Minister in late 2009, attracted formal concern from the EOC about children's rights under the Convention on the Rights of the Child, the Department's role as housing of last resort, domestic violence situations where the behaviour being complained about might involve the perpetrator, and cases where anti-social behaviour was attributable to intellectual disability or mental illness. The Committee endorsed the EOC's submission on each of these points.

Reform Area 5 — Appeals

The Housing Appeals Mechanism was restructured in September 2009. The third tier — a three-person external panel — was removed in favour of a two-tier system: senior officer review followed by a regional panel with two independent members. The Committee obtained assurances that panel members were not former Departmental employees and that training on equal opportunity law would be provided.

The remaining gap was the exclusion of termination notices from the list of appealable decisions. A tenant could appeal a tenant liability charge or a transfer decision, but not the decision to issue a notice to vacate. Recommendation 9 of the 2010 report sought to change this.

Emerging issues

Emergency housing had moved out of the Department's remit to the Department for Child Protection. The Department retained the Homeless Advisory Service (a daily-updated register of crisis accommodation), but no longer had a direct emergency housing function.

The priority waitlist was producing an increasing volume of complaints to the EOC — people whose circumstances (chronic illness, domestic violence, children at risk) had not been adequately weighted in a date-ordered system. The Commission was advocating for input into the design of any housing needs register that might replace it.

Community housing was being given greater responsibility for stock previously managed by the Department, partly as a response to Commonwealth-State funding pressures. The Committee noted that the policy reforms achieved over six years could be undermined if community housing contracts did not require equivalent standards.

From 1 July 2010, the Aboriginal Housing Legislation Amendment Act 2010 extended the Department's responsibilities to housing management in remote Aboriginal communities, under a framework of Housing Management Agreements. The scope extension was substantial. The Committee noted the need to ensure rent collection, asset protection, tenant support, and governance processes in remote communities met the standards built in the urban and regional context over the previous six years.

New recommendations (2010)

The 2010 report made 12 new recommendations:

  1. Embed a cultural awareness criterion in the role statement of all frontline and management positions, with annual performance review component.
  2. Develop strategies to recruit, retain, and promote Aboriginal staff at all levels.
  3. Commission an independent confidential survey of Aboriginal staff covering satisfaction, perceived support, and experiences of discrimination.
  4. Review cultural awareness training intervals and embed awareness in operational activities rather than induction only.
  5. Commission an independent survey of Aboriginal tenants and applicants on whether staff interactions had improved.
  6. Amend the Rental Policy Manual to remove the distinction between primary and secondary homelessness in priority assistance eligibility.
  7. Develop systems to record termination notices, vacation rates, and court applications by reason, region, and Aboriginal/non-Aboriginal status.
  8. Make analysis of policies and practices leading to eviction an ongoing priority area between the Department and the EOC.
  9. Amend the Housing Appeals Mechanism to include termination notices as an appealable decision.
  10. Include the Commissioner for Equal Opportunity in any process to revise waitlist criteria or design a housing needs register.
  11. Ensure the Disruptive Behaviour Management Strategy accounts for the concerns in the EOC's February 2010 submission.
  12. Establish an electronic data system capable of producing regular reporting on inequalities in housing outcomes.

Five monitoring points required regional offices to report quarterly on Aboriginal interpreter use and unmet need; the Department to report half-yearly on priority waitlist wait times by region and dwelling type; HSO average tenancy loads, staff training participation rates, and policy fact sheets to be tracked and conveyed to the EOC as produced.

Implementation status

Appendix 2 catalogues all 165 original recommendations. The dominant status categories are "Recommendation Supported" (agreed but not done), "Not Currently Addressed," and "Partially Implemented." Fully implemented recommendations were a minority, concentrated in structural governance changes: establishing the committee, abolishing the Fund 6 designation, adjusting some aspects of the application process. Structural changes to housing stock, staffing levels, data collection, and eviction processes mostly did not make it into the implemented column.

What changed, what didn't

The Department restructured frontline roles, rewrote letters, introduced cultural awareness training, relaxed identification requirements, abolished some discriminatory practices, and tightened the anti-social behaviour framework. The Committee's work produced a more granular picture of how Departmental policy operated in practice against Aboriginal tenants.

But eviction rates stayed disproportionate through to 2010, and the final year of data showed them getting worse, not better. The interpreter service in the Pilbara closed. The priority waiting list went from 700 to 3,000 applicants and the average wait time blew out from months to years. The data systems needed to measure inequality didn't exist. Community housing expansion raised real questions about whether the protections built over six years would carry across.

Six-monthly forums, broadened to include more advocates and housing groups, were established as the ongoing accountability mechanism after the Committee formally ceased meeting in October 2010.

Key Takeaways

  • Finding a Place (2010) is the monitoring report on a 2004 EOC inquiry into discriminatory public housing practices affecting Aboriginal people in WA.
  • Five reform areas: staffing, communication, policy and practices, maintenance and tenancy management, appeals.
  • Procedural changes were made; structural conditions producing disproportionate outcomes persisted.
  • Eviction rates for Aboriginal tenants remained disproportionate (35–63% of annual bailiff evictions) against 21.74% of the tenant population — and rose through the period.
  • Priority housing waitlist grew from ~700 applicants with a 4–6 month wait in 2004 to 3,000+ applicants with a 2+ year wait by 2010.
  • Aboriginal staffing was 7.35% of the Department's workforce against 21.74% of tenants, concentrated at junior levels.
  • The Pilbara interpreter service closed during the reporting period.
  • The Aboriginal Housing Legislation Amendment Act 2010 extended Department management to remote communities from July 2010.
  • 12 new recommendations and 5 monitoring points were made in the 2010 report; most original 165 recommendations remained unsatisfied.

Related

  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Sharp 1993 - A History of Public Housing in Western Australia
  • WA Response to Report 88 2023
  • Housing First
  • Homelessness in Western Australia

Sources

  • Equal Opportunity Commission. (2010). Finding a Place: Final Report of the Implementation and Monitoring Committee for the Section 80 Implementation and Monitoring Committee of the Inquiry into the Existence of Discriminatory Practices in Relation to the Provision of Public Housing to Aboriginal People in Western Australia. Perth: EOC. ISBN 978-09803497-4-0.
  • Beer, A. (2006). Evictions and Housing Management. AHURI.

entry.title: Wiki
entry.content:
Wiki

This is a knowledge base for my PhD research on homelessness, housing systems, and housing policy in Australia. A librarian process maintains it — reading sources, extracting key ideas, keeping things linked.

Worth saying upfront: being here doesn't mean I agree with it. I'm documenting ideas and arguments I'm actively critiquing alongside ones I find genuinely useful. The wiki covers both.

Everything is also a work in progress. Articles get updated as new sources come in, and every factual claim should trace back to one. If something isn't sourced, treat it as provisional.

Navigating

Use the file tree or search to find articles. The local graph on any article shows how concepts connect to each other. Backlinks at the bottom show what else references it.

Topics are grouped into folders. New ones appear as the wiki grows.

Topics

(Populated as articles are compiled.)

Related

  • Home
  • _master-index

entry.title: _index
entry.content:
Housing Economics

Articles in this topic cover the economics of housing — land value, alternative tenure models, and affordability policy.

Articles

Article Description
Land Rent Scheme Land rent as an alternative to freehold ownership — reducing the purchase price by separating land from dwelling
Community Land Trusts Permanently affordable housing through community ownership of land
Murray 2018 - Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing Murray's analysis of land monopoly and the case for land rent and CLT models in Australia
Pawson 2018 - Reframing Australias Housing Affordability Problem Pawson's political economy argument for why negative gearing persists despite its costs

Related Topics

  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Homelessness in Western Australia

entry.title: Pawson 2018 - Reframing Australias Housing Affordability Problem
entry.content:
Pawson 2018 — Reframing Australia's Housing Affordability Problem

Published in the Journal of Australian Political Economy (No. 81, pp. 121–43), this article by Isla Pawson challenges the dominant explanation for why negative gearing survives in Australian policy. The standard account — that it persists because of a "market-based" policy paradigm or bureaucratic inertia — is, Pawson argues, too thin. It treats the state as either captured by bad ideas or simply obstinate. The political economy account is harder to dismiss: negative gearing survives because it serves real material interests, and those interests have structural power.

The dominant narrative and its problems

Most housing scholarship frames negative gearing's durability as a policy failure — governments have been captured by a "neoliberal paradigm" that privileges market-based welfare over effective intervention. Scholars like Nicholls (2014) and Dodson (2006) trace this to the dominance of pro-market ideas within state institutions.

Pawson identifies two problems with this. First, it treats neoliberalism as an autonomous intellectual force rather than as a set of political practices emerging from material conditions. Second, it implies the state has a coherent and consistent housing policy approach, when in reality housing outcomes are shaped by taxation, welfare, finance, and planning decisions that operate across different policy domains with no unified logic. As Madden and Marcuse (2016) put it: "a historical analysis of government actions and inactions affecting housing reveals nothing of the sort" as a consistent effort to solve the housing problem.

The political economy alternative

Pawson draws on Harvey, Jessop, Peck, and Madden and Marcuse to build a structural account. The core argument has three moves.

Housing is a "spatial fix" to capital accumulation problems. As Harvey (1982) argues, when surplus capital can't find productive outlets, it flows into property — the construction of built environments becomes a way of temporarily resolving accumulation crises. Australia's housing market is deeply embedded in this logic; property is one of the economy's largest contributors to GDP, and speculative investment in housing is structurally important to Australia's current growth regime.

The state is bound to reproduce the conditions for capital accumulation. Jessop's (2002) state theory holds that capitalist social relations require something outside themselves to reproduce — the state mediates the antagonism between labour and capital and provides the conditions (education, health, housing) that capital can't produce itself. Peck (1996) extends this: the state must "make workers", including providing housing that keeps a workforce in production. Public housing is one form of this, but so are the tax settings that enable private housing investment.

Housing has undergone "hyper-commodification". Madden and Marcuse (2016) argue that housing's function as real estate now takes precedence over its function as shelter — investment value crowds out use value. Pawson applies this to Australia's context of rising prices, wage stagnation, and growing inequality, arguing that the accumulation of wealth through housing is now structurally important to the economy in a way that makes reform politically dangerous.

The numbers

Yates (2010) estimated total annual housing tax expenditures at around $53 billion. Of that, $45 billion flows to existing homeowners as imputed subsidies. Rental investors receive around $5 billion. Renters — the people who most need housing support — receive $3.2 billion.

Negative gearing specifically cost the federal budget $3.346 billion in 2013–14, roughly equivalent to total spending on jobseeker assistance and vocational training (Martin 2016).

The distributional skew within negative gearing is sharp. Coalition ministers argued that "two thirds" of negative gearers earned under $80,000 a year — a figure that sounds progressive until you note that 82% of all taxpayers are in that bracket. More informative: 29% of surgeons and anaesthetists used negative gearing in 2013–14, compared with 9% of nurses and 12% of teachers. Surgeons received an average benefit of over $3,000; nurses and teachers received under $300 (Daley and Wood 2016).

Supply didn't fix it

Between 2012 and 2016, Australian dwelling starts rose from 153,000 to 228,000 per year — a 50% increase. Australia was building more dwellings per capita than the US or UK. Over the same period, national median house prices rose 10.6% in 2016 alone (15.5% in Sydney, 13.7% in Melbourne). Rents rose over 48% nationally since the GFC.

Pawson's point is not that supply doesn't matter. It's that supply-side policy served two functions simultaneously: it gave the property development industry a social justification (they were solving the affordability problem) and it allowed the government to appear responsive to the crisis while leaving the demand-side drivers — negative gearing, capital gains discounts — intact.

Why reform is structurally difficult

Pawson ends with what Phibbs (2017) called "placebo policies" — the 2017 Budget housing package that ostensibly addressed affordability without meaningfully touching demand. This is what you'd expect from a state that is structurally reliant on property as an economic growth engine and politically reliant on the homeowner constituency and property industry donors. It's not primarily a failure of policy ideas. It's a failure that reflects who benefits from the current system and what power they hold.

The article makes no predictions about whether reform will happen. It does argue that understanding why negative gearing is durable — not just that it is — is a prerequisite for any serious challenge to it.

Key Takeaways

  • Negative gearing's durability isn't explained by bad ideas or paradigmatic inertia — it's explained by structural political economy: capital accumulation, property industry interests, and the state's dependence on housing as a growth engine.
  • Total housing tax expenditure ~$53B/year (Yates 2010): $45B to homeowners, $5B to investors, $3.2B to renters.
  • Negative gearing cost $3.346B in 2013–14 (Martin 2016) — redistributing upward, not down.
  • Supply increased 50% between 2012 and 2016; prices kept rising. Supply-side policy served investor interests, not affordability.
  • "Placebo policies" (Phibbs 2017): government appeared responsive to the housing crisis while preserving the demand-side settings that drive it.
  • Draws on Harvey (spatial fix), Jessop (capitalist state), Peck (making workers), Madden and Marcuse (hyper-commodification).

Related

  • Murray 2018 - Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing
  • Land Rent Scheme
  • Community Land Trusts
  • Public Housing in Western Australia

Sources

  • Pawson, I. (2018). Reframing Australia's housing affordability problem: The politics and economics of negative gearing. Journal of Australian Political Economy, 81, 121–143.
  • Yates, J. (2010). Tax expenditures and housing. In M. Stewart (ed.), Housing and Tax Policy. Australian Tax Research Foundation.
  • Madden, D. and Marcuse, P. (2016). In Defense of Housing. Verso.
  • Daley, J. and Wood, D. (2016). Hot Property: Negative Gearing and Capital Gains Tax Reform. Grattan Institute.

entry.title: Murray 2018 - Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing
entry.content:
Murray 2018 — Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing

Published by Prosper Australia and the Australia Institute, this report argues that Australian housing policy systematically avoids the most effective interventions because those interventions would harm existing landholders. The alternatives Murray calls "unspoken" — land rent schemes and community land trusts — are not untested or theoretical. They are well-evidenced and operating at scale internationally. The reason they are absent from mainstream Australian policy debate, Murray argues, is political rather than practical.

The core argument

Australian housing prices are, at their root, land prices. This matters because land and buildings have completely different economics. Buildings are constructed goods that depreciate over time. Land is a location — finite, non-reproducible in the ways that matter, and appreciating over time as cities grow and infrastructure improves.

The policy instruments that dominate Australian housing debate — first home buyer grants, stamp duty concessions, negative gearing, capital gains tax discounts, developer levies — all interact with land prices in ways that benefit existing landholders. First home buyer grants, for example, increase purchasing capacity without increasing supply, which drives land prices up and hands the grant value directly to sellers. Negative gearing and capital gains discounts incentivise speculative investment in land-heavy assets. Rezoning decisions create windfall gains for landholders who didn't create the value being captured.

Murray's framing draws on the classical political economy distinction between land, labour, and capital. Land value is socially created (by infrastructure, by agglomeration, by population growth) but privately captured. This is the monopoly that produces unaffordable housing, and it is what land rent schemes and CLTs are designed to interrupt.

What Murray proposes

The report makes the case for two models, both with substantial evidence bases:

Land Rent Scheme: the ACT Land Rent Scheme had 3,268 participants at the time of writing, with $69 million in cumulative savings (~$9M/year). The scheme separates land from dwelling at point of purchase, leasing the land from the Crown at an annual rate. Participants get homeownership without the land price premium.

Community Land Trusts: Murray cites the Champlain Housing Trust (Burlington, Vermont, founded 1984, 2,200+ dwellings, 26% below market), San Francisco CLT (800+ units, 47% below market), and the broader US (~250 CLTs) and UK (~280 CLTs by 2018, up from ~2 before the GFC) sectors. CLTs achieve permanent affordability through nonprofit land ownership and resale price formulas.

Why these alternatives are "unspoken"

Murray is direct about this: the political economy of Australian housing is structured around landholders. Both major parties have large constituencies of existing homeowners whose primary financial asset is their home. Policies that would moderate or reduce land prices are politically difficult to advocate, regardless of their merits for people trying to get into housing.

This is not primarily a policy design problem. The designs exist and work. It is a problem of who benefits from the status quo and how much political power they hold.

What's missing from the report

Murray's argument is strongest on the economics and the international evidence. It is less developed on how to build the political conditions for adoption — the report is a case for the policies, not a theory of change for getting them implemented.

It is also worth noting that land rent and CLT models solve affordability at point of entry and over the lifetime of a household. They don't directly address the most acute housing need — the people experiencing homelessness or in crisis accommodation who need housing now. For that, see Homelessness in WA.

Key Takeaways

  • Australian housing unaffordability is primarily a land price problem, not a building cost problem.
  • Policy instruments like FHOG, negative gearing, and capital gains discounts transfer value to existing landholders rather than improving access.
  • Land rent schemes (ACT: 3,268 households, $69M in savings) and CLTs (250+ in US, 280 in UK) are the alternatives Murray presents.
  • The alternatives are "unspoken" for political reasons, not practical ones — they are well-evidenced internationally.
  • Murray's report makes the case for the policy; it doesn't provide a theory of political change.

Related

  • Land Rent Scheme
  • Community Land Trusts
  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Homelessness in Western Australia

Sources

  • Murray, G. (2018). Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing. Prosper Australia / The Australia Institute.

entry.title: Land Rent Scheme
entry.content:
Land Rent Scheme

A land rent scheme separates ownership of land from ownership of the dwelling built on it. Buyers purchase only the house; the land is leased from a public or community body at an annual rent. Because land represents a significant portion of property prices in Australian cities, removing it from the purchase price substantially lowers the cost of entry into homeownership.

The ACT Land Rent Scheme

The most substantive Australian example is the ACT Land Rent Scheme (LRS), which Murray (2018) analyses in some detail. As of the time of Murray's report, 3,268 households were participating in the scheme. Over the life of those arrangements, participants had saved a combined $69 million compared with full freehold purchase — around $9 million per year.

The ACT scheme charges an annual land rent (the ACT government retains ownership of the land). Participants can opt out and convert to full freehold purchase, though the scheme is designed to make the rental path financially attractive enough that many don't. The land rent rate is set by formula rather than market — another way of insulating participants from speculative land price movements.

Why land value matters

Murray (2018) frames Australian housing affordability problems as fundamentally about land, not buildings. Buildings depreciate; land appreciates. The mechanisms that push up house prices — first home buyer grants, stamp duty concessions, negative gearing, developer charges, rezoning capital gains — all end up capitalised into land values. The beneficiaries are landholders, not people trying to get into housing.

A land rent scheme breaks this by removing the land component from the transaction. Participants don't pay inflated land prices upfront. They don't benefit from speculative land gains later, but they also don't carry speculative land risk. The dwelling functions more like other consumer goods: it costs what it costs to build and maintain.

Limits

The ACT has a relatively unusual tenure history — the Crown retained land ownership under the original 1911 Lease framework in ways that made the land rent scheme administratively feasible. Replicating this in WA or other states would require either large-scale land acquisition by government or alternative arrangements where government leases back land it already holds (e.g., former public housing land).

For an alternative community-based model, see Community Land Trusts, which achieves a similar affordability outcome through nonprofit land ownership rather than government land ownership.

Key Takeaways

  • Land rent schemes separate land ownership from dwelling ownership, reducing purchase prices by removing the land component.
  • The ACT Land Rent Scheme had 3,268 participants generating $69 million in cumulative savings (~$9M/year) at the time of Murray (2018).
  • Murray's argument is that Australian housing prices are fundamentally a land value problem — the mechanisms that subsidise homeownership mostly flow to landholders.
  • Replication in other states faces structural barriers around land ownership that the ACT's unusual tenure history doesn't share.

Related

  • Community Land Trusts
  • Murray 2018 - Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing
  • Public Housing in Western Australia

Sources

  • Murray, G. (2018). Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing. Prosper Australia / The Australia Institute.

entry.title: Community Land Trusts
entry.content:
Community Land Trusts

A Community Land Trust (CLT) is a nonprofit organisation that owns land permanently on behalf of a community, leasing it to households at below-market rates. Residents own their dwellings but not the land underneath. When they sell, a resale formula limits the profit they can capture — typically linking their gain to inflation or income growth rather than the full speculative market price. This is how the affordability is locked in: each resale passes the home on at below-market price to the next buyer.

The model trades speculative gain for permanent affordability. For households who want stable ownership without gambling on land prices, it's a reasonable trade.

International examples

Murray (2018) cites several examples to establish that CLTs work at scale:

Champlain Housing Trust in Burlington, Vermont, is one of the oldest and largest. Founded in 1984, it has grown to over 2,200 permanently affordable dwellings. Prices sit at around 26% below market rate on resale.

San Francisco has a smaller CLT with over 800 units, with resale prices approximately 47% below comparable market properties.

As of Murray's writing, there were around 250 CLTs operating across the United States. In the UK, the sector effectively didn't exist before the 2008 global financial crisis; by 2018 there were around 280, reflecting a decade of rapid growth driven partly by post-GFC pressure on social housing and private rental.

CLTs in Australia

The CLT model has been slow to develop in Australia. Murray (2018) attributes this partly to the political economy of housing: the mechanisms that dominate Australian housing policy (negative gearing, capital gains discounts, first home buyer grants) disproportionately benefit existing landholders and incentivise speculative ownership rather than stable, affordable tenure. CLTs are structurally at odds with this.

There have been CLT pilots in Australia, including in Western Australia, but the sector is not at the scale seen in the US or UK.

How CLTs differ from public housing and private ownership

Public housing provides secure tenure but depends on ongoing government funding and carries the political risk of asset sales. Private ownership creates equity but exposes households to land price speculation and prices people out of stable communities when markets heat up. CLTs sit between these: permanent tenure security, below-market prices, and community governance, but without the state-dependency of public housing and without the speculative exposure of private ownership.

This positions them as a potential complement to public housing rather than a replacement — serving households whose incomes are too high for public housing but too low for the private market in high-price areas.

For the land economics argument underlying CLTs, see Land Rent Scheme and Murray 2018 - Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing.

Key Takeaways

  • CLTs own land permanently through a nonprofit, leasing it to households who own their dwellings.
  • Resale formulas limit profit capture and lock in affordability across generations.
  • Major examples: Champlain Housing Trust (2,200+ dwellings, 26% below market); San Francisco CLT (800+ dwellings, 47% below market); ~250 US CLTs; UK sector grew from ~2 to ~280 in the decade after 2008.
  • CLTs are underdeveloped in Australia, partly because they cut against the land-speculative incentives dominating Australian housing policy.
  • Positioned as a complement to public housing, not a replacement — targeting moderate-income households priced out of the private market.

Related

  • Land Rent Scheme
  • Murray 2018 - Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing
  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Housing First

Sources

  • Murray, G. (2018). Unspoken Alternatives to Expensive Housing. Prosper Australia / The Australia Institute.

entry.title: _index
entry.content:
Homelessness in Western Australia

Articles in this topic cover homelessness policy, mortality outcomes, and government responses in Western Australia.

Articles

Article Description
Housing First Overview of the Housing First model and its application in WA
Homeless Mortality Mortality rates and causes of death among people experiencing homelessness
WA Response to Report 88 2023 WA Government's response to the parliamentary inquiry into homelessness services funding
Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth BMJ Open cohort study tracking deaths of people experiencing homelessness in Perth, 2020–2022

Related Topics

  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Housing Economics

entry.title: WA Response to Report 88 2023
entry.content:
WA Response to Report 88 (2023)

Report 88 — Funding of Homelessness Services in Western Australia — was the final report of the Standing Committee on Estimates and Financial Operations, released on 22 June 2023. The inquiry was initiated in November 2021, ran for 19 months, received 48 submissions, and held 27 public hearings. It made 57 recommendations addressed to government agencies.

The WA Government's response to those 57 recommendations is the document this article examines.

The headline figures

The government's response centred on its $2.6 billion four-year investment in social housing and homelessness measures — a figure the response describes as a record. The investment includes delivering 4,000 new social homes. At the time of the response, 1,356 new social homes had been added since July 2021, with more than 1,100 under contract or construction.

Homelessness services specifically receive around $225 million per year. This funds community sector organisations delivering specialist homelessness services across WA.

Specific programmes

Several specific initiatives are named in the response:

All Paths Lead to Home (2020–2030) is the overarching state strategy, incorporating Housing First principles.

Djuripiny Mia is a 100-home development with wraparound support, designed for people with complex needs.

Common Ground developments in East Perth and Mandurah provide permanent supportive housing, combining social housing tenants from the waiting list with people experiencing homelessness.

The Housing First Homelessness Initiative targets rough sleepers with assertive outreach and immediate housing placement.

HEART (Homeless Emergency and Response Team) provides outreach in the inner city.

The By Name List — a real-time registry of people experiencing homelessness in a given area — received $1.3 million in funding via the Australian Alliance to End Homelessness. It is designed to coordinate service responses to individuals rather than operating through general programme eligibility.

Commonwealth funding and the NHHA

The response gives significant attention to the National Housing and Homelessness Agreement (NHHA), which began in July 2018 and governs Commonwealth funding to the states for housing and homelessness. WA's formal position is that the Commonwealth must do more: the previous NHHA was a 50/50 cost-sharing arrangement that a 2022 Productivity Commission review found hadn't driven wider housing policy reform. The bilateral schedule expired June 2023, with a one-year extension in place while negotiations continued.

The response frames multiple drivers of homelessness — income support levels, welfare settings, tax treatment of housing investment — as primarily Commonwealth responsibilities. This is accurate on the formal division of powers, though it also functions as a way of managing expectations about what the state can solve alone.

What the response doesn't address

The 57 recommendations cover a broader range than the response's headline investment figures suggest. The response accepts, accepts in principle, or notes the recommendations without providing granular implementation timelines for all of them. The gap between funding announcements and what actually changes in service delivery for people experiencing homelessness is a recurring issue in WA homelessness policy.

The supply question is real and serious. A housing system that is $2.6 billion over four years ahead of where it was is still operating in a context of historically low vacancy rates in the private rental market and a public housing waiting list that measures in years for most applicants.

For the mortality context that makes this urgent, see Homeless Mortality and Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth.

Key Takeaways

  • Report 88 (June 2023) made 57 recommendations following a 19-month inquiry into WA homelessness services funding.
  • The WA Government committed $2.6 billion over four years, including 4,000 new social homes and $225 million/year in homelessness services.
  • Key programmes: All Paths Lead to Home, Djuripiny Mia, Common Ground (East Perth + Mandurah), Housing First Homelessness Initiative, HEART outreach, By Name List.
  • The NHHA (Commonwealth-state funding agreement) is a live political issue; WA argues the Commonwealth must increase its contribution.
  • The gap between commitment and delivery, and between new supply and actual waiting list demand, remains significant.

Related

  • Housing First
  • Homeless Mortality
  • Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth
  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Housing Economics

Sources

  • WA Government. (2023). West Australian Government Response to Report 88: Funding of Homelessness Services in Western Australia. Perth: Government of Western Australia.

entry.title: Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth
entry.content:
Tuson et al. 2024 — Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth

This is a population-based dynamic cohort study tracking deaths among people experiencing homelessness in Perth, Western Australia, between 2020 and 2022. Published in BMJ Open (2024;14:e081260), it is one of the first studies to systematically quantify homeless mortality in WA using linked administrative data.

Method

The cohort was built from multiple administrative datasets — not a survey or self-report sample. Participants were identified through their contact with homelessness services. Death was verified for 93% of cases through two or more data sources, which is a methodological strength: linked data studies of this kind often rely on single-source verification that can miss or misattribute deaths.

The cohort comprised 8,753 individuals. 360 deaths were recorded during the 2020–2022 period.

Key findings

The median age of death in the cohort was 50 years. The median age of death in the Australian general population is 82 years. That is a 32-year gap.

73% of those who died were male. 30% were Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander — against approximately 3.5% Aboriginal/Torres Strait Islander in the general WA population.

The study covers 2020–2022, which includes the COVID-19 pandemic period. The authors note this context, though they do not attribute the mortality patterns primarily to COVID-19.

What the numbers mean

A 32-year gap in median age of death is not a marginal finding. It suggests that homelessness in Perth is not a temporary disadvantage that people pass through — for a substantial portion of this cohort, it is a terminal condition. The people dying in this sample are dying in their forties and fifties from conditions that, in the general population, are managed across decades of continuous healthcare.

The Aboriginal overrepresentation is stark and not explained by the study itself — the study identifies it, not its causes. The explanation requires a broader historical and structural account: dispossession, inadequate housing supply in remote and regional WA, discrimination in the public housing system (documented in Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010), and persistent poverty. See Homeless Mortality for more on this.

Significance for policy

The study provides a quantitative anchor for advocacy around homelessness funding in WA. The 2023 parliamentary inquiry (Report 88) and the government's response (see WA Response to Report 88 2023) both drew on evidence of mortality outcomes to justify the scale of investment committed.

The gap between the mortality data and what current policy commitments can realistically address is large. A 32-year gap in life expectancy is not closed by a single government investment cycle, particularly when the supply of housing is the binding constraint and the services needed to support people once housed are separately resourced.

Limitations

The cohort covers only people who accessed formal homelessness services — people who are entirely outside the service system are not captured. This means the 8,753 figure and the death counts are floor estimates for the people experiencing homelessness in Perth over this period. The actual number of people experiencing homelessness, and the actual number of deaths, is likely higher.

Key Takeaways

  • Cohort of 8,753 people experiencing homelessness in Perth, 2020–2022; 360 deaths recorded.
  • Median age of death: 50 years, against 82 years in the general Australian population.
  • 73% of deaths were male; 30% were Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander.
  • 93% of deaths verified via two or more data sources — robust linked-data methodology.
  • Study covers the COVID-19 period but does not attribute the mortality patterns primarily to COVID-19.
  • Cohort is limited to people who accessed formal services — deaths outside the service system are not counted.

Related

  • Homeless Mortality
  • Housing First
  • WA Response to Report 88 2023
  • Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010

Sources

  • Tuson, M., et al. (2024). Tracking deaths of people experiencing homelessness in Perth, Western Australia, using a population-based dynamic cohort. BMJ Open, 14, e081260.

entry.title: Housing First
entry.content:
Housing First

Housing First is a homelessness intervention model built on a simple reversal of the conventional approach: give people a stable home first, then address whatever else is going on. The traditional approach — often called the "treatment first" or "staircase" model — required people to demonstrate sobriety, mental health stability, or compliance with support programmes before they could access permanent housing. Housing First treats stable housing as a precondition for recovery, not a reward for it.

The model originated with Sam Tsemberis and Pathways to Housing in New York in the 1990s. It has since been implemented across North America, Europe, and Australia, with a growing evidence base showing that it reduces chronic homelessness more effectively than staircase alternatives.

Housing First in WA

The WA Government's All Paths Lead to Home strategy (2020–2030) incorporates Housing First principles as a central plank of its homelessness response. The government has established several Housing First–style programmes as part of a $2.6 billion investment in social housing and homelessness services across four years.

Specific initiatives include:

Djuripiny Mia is a 100-home social housing development with wraparound support services. The name is Noongar for "place of healing" and the development is explicitly designed for people with complex needs.

The Common Ground model — permanent supportive housing with on-site services — has been implemented in East Perth and Mandurah. Common Ground combines affordable housing for a mixed population (around half tenants from the social housing waiting list, half from street homelessness) with 24/7 support staff.

The Housing First Homelessness Initiative targets rough sleepers and those in crisis accommodation with assertive outreach, immediate housing placement, and voluntary support services.

Limits and tensions

Housing First doesn't eliminate the need for support services — it changes the sequencing. People housed through Housing First still need access to mental health care, drug and alcohol services, income support, and social connection. The question is whether those services are available once someone is housed, which depends on resourcing that Housing First funding doesn't necessarily cover.

There is also a supply constraint that Housing First cannot resolve on its own. The model works best when there are enough dwellings to house people quickly. In WA, where the public housing waiting list is long and the private rental market has historically low vacancy rates, the "housing" part of Housing First requires serious supply expansion — not just a reallocation of what exists.

For WA's current policy commitments on supply, see WA Response to Report 88 2023. For the mortality context that makes this urgent, see Homeless Mortality and Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth.

Key Takeaways

  • Housing First prioritises stable housing before treatment or behavioural compliance — the opposite of the staircase model.
  • WA's All Paths Lead to Home (2020–2030) adopts Housing First as a framework.
  • Djuripiny Mia (100 homes), Common Ground (East Perth and Mandurah), and the Housing First Homelessness Initiative are the main WA implementations.
  • Housing First doesn't resolve the underlying supply shortage or the services gap once people are housed.

Related

  • Homeless Mortality
  • WA Response to Report 88 2023
  • Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth
  • Public Housing in Western Australia
  • Community Land Trusts

Sources

  • WA Government. (2023). West Australian Government Response to Report 88: Funding of Homelessness Services in Western Australia. Perth: Government of Western Australia.

entry.title: Homeless Mortality
entry.content:
Homeless Mortality

People experiencing homelessness die significantly earlier than the general population. The causes are partly direct — exposure, violence, overdose — but the bulk of the gap comes from chronic disease and a healthcare system that effectively excludes people without stable housing. In Perth, a 2024 cohort study found a median age of death of 50 years, against 82 years for the general Australian population.

What the data shows

Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth tracked a cohort of 8,753 people who accessed homelessness services in Perth between 2020 and 2022. 360 died during the study period — 73% male, 30% Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander. That last figure is striking: Aboriginal people are around 3.5% of the general WA population but 30% of deaths in this cohort.

Cause-of-death data from the study hasn't been fully extracted here, but the pattern in comparable studies is consistent: cardiovascular disease, chronic liver disease, cancer, and drug- and alcohol-related deaths dominate, along with external causes (suicide, injury, assault). The common thread is that all of these are conditions that benefit from stable housing, continuous medical care, and reliable access to food, warmth, and sanitation.

The Aboriginal dimension

The overrepresentation of Aboriginal people in homeless mortality data in WA isn't incidental. It reflects the compounding of structural disadvantage: historical dispossession, disproportionate poverty, inadequate housing supply in remote and regional areas, and documented discrimination in the public housing system (see Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010). A person who is Aboriginal, homeless, and living in WA faces a system that has historically failed them at multiple levels simultaneously.

Why housing matters for mortality

The relationship between housing and health is not subtle. Stable housing enables consistent access to healthcare. It allows chronic conditions to be managed rather than presenting at crisis point in emergency departments. It provides the physical conditions — warmth, sleep, nutrition — that determine baseline health. Without it, the healthcare system becomes an episodic emergency service rather than a system capable of managing long-term health.

This is part of the evidence base for Housing First — the argument that you cannot meaningfully improve health outcomes for people experiencing homelessness without first addressing the housing instability that prevents health improvement.

Policy response

The mortality picture has been one of the drivers of pressure on WA Government to expand its homelessness response. The 2023 parliamentary inquiry (Report 88) and the government's formal response (see WA Response to Report 88 2023) both acknowledge the urgency. Whether the current policy commitments — $2.6 billion over four years, 4,000 new social homes, expanded services — are adequate to shift these outcomes is a separate question.

Key Takeaways

  • People experiencing homelessness in Perth have a median age of death of 50 years, against 82 for the general population (Tuson et al., 2024).
  • 30% of deaths in the Perth cohort were Aboriginal or Torres Strait Islander, against ~3.5% in the general WA population.
  • Deaths are driven by chronic disease, substance-related conditions, and external causes — all conditions influenced by housing stability.
  • The Aboriginal overrepresentation reflects compounding structural disadvantage, including documented discrimination in public housing access.
  • Housing First is the main policy framework for addressing this, but supply constraints limit its reach.

Related

  • Tuson et al. 2024 - Tracking Deaths of Homeless People Perth
  • Housing First
  • WA Response to Report 88 2023
  • Finding a Place - EOC Report 2010
  • Public Housing in Western Australia

Sources

  • Tuson, M., et al. (2024). Tracking deaths of people experiencing homelessness in Perth, Western Australia, using a population-based dynamic cohort. BMJ Open, 14, e081260.

entry.title: What is Deconstructing Homelessness?
entry.content:
8 week course examining the structural causes of, and responses to, homelessness in Australia.

This isn’t the usual way Australia discusses homelessness.

Deconstructing Homelessness challenges the narrative, history, funding, research, policies and approaches to homelessness in Australia, while answering the question why does homelessness persist.

This course is related to and informed by Matt Vapor’s PhD research.

Course Outline

Week 1:

Homelessness and Empathy

Week 2:

Observations from the Adversarial Perspective

Week 3:

Managing the Outhouse to the Sh!thouse: Housing Systems from Federation

Week 4:

Economics Behind Australia's Current Housing System

Week 5:

Low Down on the Flow Down: Homelessness Funding Dynamics in Australia

Week 6:

Equity and Agency: A Critical Analysis of Housing First in Western Australia

Week 7:

Sociology of Homelessness: Dehumanisation and the Dynamics of Fear

Week 8

Solidifying the System: Australia's Housing Future

Here’s what the attendees have said:

“I was a little intimidated at first, thinking I may not be able to keep up with the intellectual language, this was not an issue. I felt a part of the session."

“This is something unique and ground breaking. It’s a blend of perspectives, of someone that’s experienced homelessness, become a phenomenal researcher and teacher, and is using his experiences and skills to dissect the structures that perpetuate homelessness.”

“The more Matt deconstructs homelessness through this course, the more it feels like I can see the wool lifting from my eyes. The evidence has been right there from the start. Matt has been able to pull together publicly available information to inform contemporary knowledge of homelessness & how the current system is broken.”

“It was refreshing to see and hear an honest, critical analysis of Housing Policy that didn't adhere to the usual narrative.”

“Each week is a continual process, opening up a deeper understanding that the system isn't just broken. The problem isn't about fixing a broken system, the system has never been right for it to break.”
